title: Thai Mango Sticky Rice

ingredients.name: glutinous (sticky) rice, soaked 4 hours or overnight
visual_checks: also called sweet rice or khao niao; soak before starting
ingredients.notes: also called sweet rice or khao niao; soak before starting

subs.name: short-grain sushi rice for a slightly different texture
subs.notes: sushi rice works in a pinch; sticky rice gives a more authentic chewy result

ingredients.name: ripe alphonso or kesar mangoes
visual_checks: must be fully ripe and sweet
ingredients.notes: must be fully ripe and sweet

subs.name: ripe papaya slices or fresh lychees
subs.notes: papaya is more widely available and equally complementary

subs.name: a drizzle of coconut cream and a mint leaf for garnish

steps.text: Rinse the soaked glutinous rice until the water runs clear, then drain. Line a steamer basket with a damp muslin cloth or cheesecloth and spread the rice in an even layer. Steam over boiling water on medium heat for 25-30 minutes until the grains are translucent and tender but still slightly chewy — a grain pressed between fingers should be fully soft with no hard white centre.

While the rice steams, gently heat the coconut milk in a small saucepan over low heat. Add 3 tablespoons of sugar and 0.25 teaspoon of salt, stirring until dissolved. Do not boil — just warm enough to dissolve the sugar, about 3 minutes. Remove from heat and divide into two portions: roughly 300 ml for soaking the rice, and 100 ml reserved for the topping sauce.

Transfer the hot steamed rice to a large mixing bowl immediately. Pour the 300 ml portion of sweetened coconut milk over the hot rice and fold gently to coat every grain. Cover tightly with a plate or cling film and rest for 20-25 minutes — the rice will absorb the coconut milk and become glossy, soft and fragrant.

Make the salted coconut topping sauce: to the reserved 100 ml of coconut milk, add the remaining 1 tablespoon of sugar and 0.25 teaspoon of salt. Stir over low heat for 1 minute until the sugar dissolves. This thin, salty-sweet sauce is the finishing touch.

Peel the mangoes and slice the flesh cleanly off the stone in smooth, long cuts. Fan the slices out on a plate or arrange alongside a generous mound of the soaked sticky rice. Drizzle the salted coconut topping sauce over the rice, scatter toasted sesame seeds on top if using, and serve at room temperature. The dish does not keep well; eat within 2 hours.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: mango → ripe papaya slices or fresh lychees (papaya is more widely available and equally complementary); rice → short-grain sushi rice for a slightly different texture (sushi rice works in a pinch; sticky rice gives a more authentic chewy result); sesame-seeds → a drizzle of coconut cream and a mint leaf for garnish
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Steam the rice in an idli steamer lined with a damp muslin cloth — this works excellently. Sticky (glutinous) rice is sold as 'sweet rice' or 'glutinous rice' in Indian Asian grocery stores and online (Amazon India). Indian alphonso, kesar or banganapalli mangoes in season are arguably better than Thai varieties and work perfectly. All other ingredients are readily available in India.
